target.title: Node run/install: add --header for WebSocket upgrade (Cloudflare Zero Trust)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `openclaw node run` and `node install` had no way to pass custom HTTP headers on the WebSocket upgrade request, causing silent 302 redirects and connection failures when the node gateway sits behind Cloudflare Zero Trust or similar auth proxies
- **Why it matters:** Teams deploying OpenClaw in Zero Trust network environments (common in enterprise) were completely blocked — the WS handshake would redirect instead of upgrading, with no workaround
- **What changed:** Added a repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` CLI flag to both `node run` and `node install`; headers are persisted to config and forwarded to the gateway WebSocket client; also supports `OPENCLAW_NODE_HEADERS`, `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID`, and `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET` env vars for the installed daemon service
- **What did NOT change:** No changes to gateway routing logic, auth token handling, skill execution, or any behavior for users not passing `--header`

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #42792
- Related: none

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `openclaw node run` now accepts repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` flags
- `openclaw node install` now accepts repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` flags; headers are written to config and used by the installed daemon on startup
- New env vars supported: `OPENCLAW_NODE_HEADERS` (JSON), `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID`, `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET`
- No behavior change for users who do not pass `--header`

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `Yes` — headers may contain auth tokens; persisted to existing node config file (same location/permissions as all other config)
- New/changed network calls? `Yes` — user-supplied headers forwarded on outbound WebSocket upgrade request
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- **Risk + mitigation:** Headers containing secrets are written to the existing node config file (not a new location). No headers are logged or surfaced in output. The `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET` env var name makes sensitivity clear to users.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.x (Apple Silicon)
- Runtime/container: Node.js v22, pnpm monorepo
- Model/provider: N/A (gateway connectivity issue, not model-specific)
- Integration/channel: Cloudflare Zero Trust (simulated via mock HTTP proxy)
- Relevant config: none required

### Steps

**1. Start a mock proxy that returns 302 if the CF header is absent:**
```js
const http = require('http');
const server = http.createServer((req, res) => {
  if (!req.headers['cf-access-client-id']) {
    res.writeHead(302, { Location: 'https://example.cloudflareaccess.com' });
    res.end();
    return;
  }
  res.writeHead(200);
  res.end();
});
server.listen(9999, () => console.log('Mock proxy listening on 9999'));
```

**2. Without fix — run in second terminal:**
```bash
pnpm openclaw node run --host localhost --port 9999
```

**3. With fix — run with headers:**
```bash
pnpm openclaw node run --host localhost --port 9999 \
  --header "CF-Access-Client-Id: test123" \
  --header "CF-Access-Client-Secret: secret456"
```

### Expected

- WS upgrade request includes supplied headers
- No 302 redirect when correct Zero Trust headers are present

### Actual (before fix)

- WS upgrade sent with no custom headers → Cloudflare returns 302 → `Unexpected server response: 302`

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets

**Before fix:**
```
Unexpected server response: 302
```

**After fix:**
```
Unexpected server response: 200
```
_(200 is expected — mock is not a real WS server; the point is the 302 redirect is gone)_

New unit tests added:
- `src/gateway/client.test.ts` — headers passed to WS constructor
- `src/daemon/program-args.test.ts` — `--header` args emitted correctly
- `src/cli/node-cli/header-args.test.ts` — parse logic including edge cases

All 895 test files, 7301 tests passing. `pnpm check` clean.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:**
  - `node run` with single `--header` → header present in WS upgrade
  - `node run` with multiple `--header` flags → all headers forwarded
  - `node run` with no `--header` → behavior identical to pre-fix
  - Mock proxy 302→200 transition confirmed end-to-end
  - `pnpm check` and `pnpm test` both clean on branch
- **Edge cases checked:**
  - Malformed header string (missing `:`) → `parseHeaderArgs` throws with clear error
  - Header value containing `:` (e.g. URLs) → splits on first colon only, value preserved
  - Empty header value → accepted (valid HTTP)
- **What I did not verify:**
  - Live Cloudflare Zero Trust environment (tested via mock proxy only)
  - `node install` daemon persistence across system restarts
  - Windows path behavior for config persistence

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — `--header` is purely additive; existing behavior unchanged
- Config/env changes? `Yes` — new optional `headers` field in node config; old configs without it continue to work
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert: remove `--header` flags from invocation, or delete `headers` key from node config file
- Files/config to restore: `~/.openclaw/node-config.json` — remove the `headers` field
- Known bad symptoms: WS connections failing after upgrade if headers are malformed; `parseHeaderArgs` throws at startup with a descriptive error

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** User accidentally persists a sensitive token to disk via `node install --header`
  - **Mitigation:** Consistent with how other sensitive config (API keys) is already handled in node config. No new attack surface; file permissions unchanged.
- **Risk:** Header injection via malformed `--header` value
  - **Mitigation:** `parseHeaderArgs` validates format; `ws` library handles header serialization — no raw string concatenation into HTTP stream.

target.title: Nostr: restore DM delivery path

## Summary
- pass a single DM subscription filter object to `subscribeMany()`
- keep `gateway.startAccount()` alive until abort instead of resolving immediately
- add a `sendMedia` fallback so outbound delivery can send attachment links over DM

## Root cause
`main` still had all three failures described in #32252:
1. `nostr-bus.ts` wrapped the DM filter in an array before calling `subscribeMany()`.
2. `channel.ts` resolved `startAccount()` immediately after startup instead of waiting for abort.
3. the outbound adapter only exposed `sendText`, so the generic delivery layer treated Nostr outbound as unconfigured.

## Testing
- `pnpm test extensions/nostr/src/channel.outbound.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/nostr/src/nostr-bus.subscribe.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/nostr/src/channel.test.ts`

Closes #32252

target.title: fix(feishu): convert Markdown tables to native table components in cards
target.body:
## Problem

Feishu's Card JSON 2.0 `markdown` element does **not** support standard Markdown table syntax (`| col | col |`). When `buildMarkdownCard()` wraps table-containing text in a single `{"tag": "markdown"}` element, the tables render as broken plain text in Feishu messages.

This means that when `renderMode=card` (or `auto` with detected tables), the card path is taken but tables are unreadable.

### Before (broken)

Tables in card messages appear as raw pipe-separated text:

```
| Name | Score |
|------|-------|
| Alice | 95 |
| Bob | 87 |
```

### After (fixed)

Tables are rendered as native Feishu table components with proper headers, alignment, and row data — matching the official Feishu card design system.

## Solution

Introduces a new `markdown-card.ts` module (`extensions/feishu/src/`) that:

1. **Auto-detects** Markdown tables using a regex that matches header + separator + data rows
2. **Converts** each table to a native Feishu `table` component (Card JSON 2.0) with:
   - Properly typed columns (`display_name`, `data_type`, `width`)
   - Header styling (bold, centered, grey background)
   - Row data mapped to column names
3. **Splits** mixed content into alternating `markdown` + `table` elements
4. **Falls back** gracefully when table parsing fails (keeps raw text)

The existing `buildMarkdownCard()` in `send.ts` now delegates to `buildMarkdownCardWithTables()`, maintaining full backward compatibility.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `extensions/feishu/src/markdown-card.ts` | **New** — table detection, parsing, and card builder |
| `extensions/feishu/src/markdown-card.test.ts` | **New** — 19 unit tests |
| `extensions/feishu/src/send.ts` | Updated `buildMarkdownCard()` to use the new builder |
| `extensions/feishu/src/send.test.ts` | Added 2 integration tests for `buildMarkdownCard` |

## Test plan

- [x] 19 new unit tests for `parseTableRow`, `mdTableToFeishuTable`, `splitTextAndTables`, `hasMarkdownTables`, `buildMarkdownCardWithTables`
- [x] 2 integration tests for `buildMarkdownCard` (plain text + table conversion)
- [x] All existing `send.test.ts` tests pass (6/6)
- [x] All existing `outbound.test.ts` tests pass (13/13)
- [x] All existing `streaming-card.test.ts` tests pass (7/7)

## References

- [Feishu Card JSON 2.0 Structure](https://open.feishu.cn/document/feishu-cards/card-json-v2-structure)
- [Feishu Markdown Tags](https://open.feishu.cn/document/common-capabilities/message-card/message-cards-content/using-markdown-tags)
- [Feishu Table Component](https://open.feishu.cn/document/feishu-cards/card-json-v2-components/content-components/table)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(cron): prevent duplicate job execution after gateway restart (#42640)
target.body:
Fixes #42640

## Summary

After gateway restart, the cron scheduler re-executed jobs that already ran before the restart, causing duplicate deliveries. The root cause: `isRunnableJob` treated stale `nextRunAtMs <= now` slots as due without checking whether they had already been executed.

## Changes

- Add `hasAlreadyExecutedScheduledSlot` guard in `timer.ts`: if `lastRunAtMs >= nextRunAtMs`, the expired slot was already executed — skip it
- Preserves missed-slot fallthrough so genuinely missed jobs still get caught up

## Testing

- Added regression test: `does not re-run an already-executed stale due slot on restart`
- 501 tests pass across cron module

target.title: fix(skills): list omitted skill names when prompt is truncated
target.body:
## Problem

When many skills are installed (e.g. 45+), the prompt truncation at `maxSkillsPromptChars` (default 30KB) silently drops skills near the end of the alphabetically-sorted list. The agent sees only a generic `⚠️ Skills truncated: included N of M` message but has **no way to discover which skills were omitted** — it cannot use them even when directly relevant to the user's request.

Real-world example: a user sends a `mp.weixin.qq.com` link, but the `wechat-reader` skill (w-prefix, sorted last) is truncated out. The agent falls back to `curl`, which fails because WeChat articles are JS-rendered.

## Solution

When skills are truncated, the note now includes the **names** of omitted skills (capped at 1KB to stay compact) and suggests `openclaw skills info <name>` or `openclaw skills list` so the agent can still discover and read them on demand.

**Before:**
```
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 40 of 45. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.
```

**After:**
```
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 40 of 45. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit. Additional installed skills not expanded here: verify-on-browser, wechat-reader, web-search-plus, wrangler, video-frames. If a listed skill name becomes relevant, inspect it with `openclaw skills info <name>` or `openclaw skills list` before deciding whether to read its SKILL.md.
```

## Changes

- `applySkillsPromptLimits()` now returns `omittedSkills` alongside the existing fields
- New `formatTruncatedSkillNames()` builds a compact name list (hard-capped at 1KB)
- New `buildSkillsTruncationNote()` assembles the enriched truncation message
- Two new test cases covering name listing for both char-based and count-based truncation

## Tests

All 34 existing + new tests pass (`npx vitest run src/agents/skills`).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Skills prompt truncation should keep the emitted prompt within `maxSkillsPromptChars` and advertise omitted skill identifiers that `openclaw skills info <name>` can resolve exactly, including raw keys with characters such as `<` and backticks.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Darwin arm64, Node v22.22.2, pnpm 10.33.2, branch `fix/skills-truncation-visibility`, current HEAD `db926508c4`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Rendered 80 synthetic workspace skills through the real `buildWorkspaceSkillsPrompt()` code path with `maxSkillsPromptChars: 520` and raw key `skill-00<zero`raw`; then parsed the emitted `<omitted_skill_keys>` payload and resolved the first advertised key through `formatSkillInfo()` exact lookup. For comparison, the same rendering script was also run against local worktree commit `79acf3289d` before the budgeted lookup-key note.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output copied from the local command run.

```text
Before (79acf3289d):
{
  "commit": "before",
  "cap": 520,
  "length": 989,
  "hasOmittedSkillKeys": false,
  "advertisedCount": 0,
  "advertisedFirst": null,
  "hasRawKey": false
}
--- prompt excerpt ---
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 0 of 80 (compact format, descriptions omitted). Omitted: skill-00zeroraw, skill-01, skill-02, skill-03, skill-04, skill-05, skill-06, skill-07, skill-08, skill-09, skill-10, skill-11, skill-12, skill-13, skill-14, skill-15, skill-16, skill-17, skill-18, skill-19, skill-20, skill-21, skill-22, skill-23, skill-24, skill-25, skill-26, skill-27, skill-28, skill-29, skill-30, skill-31, skill-32, skill-33, skill-34, skill-35, skill-36, skill-37, skill-38, skill-39, skill-40, skill-41, skill-42, skill-43, skill-44, skill-45, skill-46, skill-47, skill-48, skill-49, skill-50, skill-51, skill-52, skill-53, skill-54, skill-55, skill-56, skill-57, skill-58, skill-59, skill-60, skill-61, skill-62, skill-63, skill-64, skill-65, skill-66, skill-67, skill-68, skill-69, skill-70, skill-71, skill-72, skill-73, skill-74, skill-75, skill-76, skill-77, skill-78, skill-79. Use `openclaw skills info <name>` to load them on demand. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.

After (db926508c4):
{
  "commit": "after",
  "cap": 520,
  "length": 512,
  "hasOmittedSkillKeys": true,
  "advertisedCount": 21,
  "advertisedFirst": "skill-00<zero`raw",

Omitted skill lookup keys for `openclaw skills info <name>` (JSON data; use exact string values): <omitted_skill_keys>["skill-00\u003czero`raw","skill-01","skill-02","skill-03","skill-04","skill-05","skill-06","skill-07","skill-08","skill-09","skill-10","skill-11","skill-12","skill-13","skill-14","skill-15","skill-16","skill-17","skill-18","skill-19","skill-20"]</omitted_skill_keys> (+59 more) Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.
```

Observed result after fix: The current prompt is 512 chars, below the 520 char cap, includes `<omitted_skill_keys>`, preserves the raw advertised key as JSON data, and `formatSkillInfo()` resolves the first advertised key exactly as `skill-00<zero`raw`.

target.title: fix: route embedded tool calls through in-process dispatch (#40237)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #40237 (also resolves #5703 and #6508 which were circular-duped shut).

When agent tools like `cron.run`, `cron.list`, `cron.add` are called from within an active LLM session, `callGatewayTool` opens a **new WebSocket connection** to the same gateway. Because the gateway's Node.js event loop is busy processing the current LLM turn, the second WS connection sits in queue and times out after 10s.

This PR routes embedded tool calls through the existing in-process dispatch path (`dispatchGatewayMethod`) when running inside the gateway process, bypassing WS entirely. External CLI tool calls continue to use WS unchanged.

## Changes

- **`src/gateway/local-dispatch.ts`** (new): lightweight registry that the gateway server populates at startup. Exposes `tryLocalGatewayDispatch` which returns a promise when in-process dispatch is available, or `undefined` when running outside the gateway (e.g. CLI).

- **`src/gateway/server-plugins.ts`**: registers `dispatchGatewayMethod` as the local dispatcher when `setFallbackGatewayContext` is called during gateway startup.

- **`src/agents/tools/gateway.ts`**: `callGatewayTool` now checks `tryLocalGatewayDispatch` first. If available (inside gateway), dispatches in-process with zero WS overhead. Otherwise falls through to the existing WS path.

## How it works

```
CLI tool call (external):
  callGatewayTool → tryLocalGatewayDispatch returns undefined → WS path (unchanged)

Embedded tool call (inside gateway LLM session):
  callGatewayTool → tryLocalGatewayDispatch returns Promise → dispatchGatewayMethod
    → handleGatewayRequest (in-process, no WS) → immediate response
```

The `dispatchGatewayMethod` function already exists and is battle-tested — it's used by plugin subagent runtime for sessions, agent dispatch, etc. This PR simply makes it available to agent tools via the registry pattern.

## Test plan

- [x] Unit tests for `local-dispatch.ts` (registry behavior, reset, error propagation)
- [x] Integration tests in `gateway.test.ts` verifying:
  - Embedded tool calls use in-process dispatch (no WS `callGateway` mock called)
  - External tool calls fall back to WS when no dispatcher registered
  - `undefined` params correctly normalized to `{}`
- [x] Existing `server-plugins.test.ts` tests pass (5/5)
- [x] Type check clean (`pnpm tsgo`)
- [x] Lint/format clean (`pnpm check`)

target.title: fix(discord): preserve mentionedUsers on debounced synthetic messages
target.body:
## Summary

- When multiple Discord messages are debounced (e.g. chunked bot replies), the synthetic message created via object spread (`{ ...last.data.message }`) loses Carbon `Message` prototype getters (`mentionedUsers`, `mentionedRoles`, `mentionedEveryone`)
- This causes bot-to-bot @mention detection to always fail for multi-chunk messages, since `explicitlyMentioned` evaluates to `false` on the plain object
- Fix: materialise mention data from all debounced entries into the synthetic message object

## Root cause

`src/discord/monitor/message-handler.ts` creates a synthetic message when debouncing multiple inbound messages into one:

```ts
const syntheticMessage = {
  ...last.data.message,   // ← Carbon Message instance; spread copies own props only
  content: combinedBaseText,
  // mentionedUsers getter lives on the prototype → lost after spread
};
```

In `preflightDiscordMessage`, `message.mentionedUsers` returns `undefined` instead of the mention array, so:
1. `explicitlyMentioned = Boolean(botId && message.mentionedUsers?.some(...))` → `false`
2. Text-based regex also fails because `resolveDiscordMentions` already rewrote `<@ID>` → `@name` in the combined text
3. Result: "no-mention" skip for every bot-to-bot @mention in chunked messages

User-to-bot mentions work because single messages (`entries.length === 1`) pass through the original Carbon Message instance.

## Test plan

- [ ] Bot A sends a multi-chunk message mentioning Bot B → Bot B should now detect the mention and respond
- [ ] Single-message mentions (user→bot, bot→bot) continue to work
- [ ] Messages with no mentions still get "no-mention" skip as expected

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat(models): expose provider rate-limit headroom in probe results
target.body:
## Summary

Adds rate-limit headroom information to `openclaw models status --probe` output. When `--rate-limits` is passed, each successful probe result includes remaining RPM/TPM data extracted from provider HTTP response headers.

Closes #42401

## Changes

### New types (`list.types.ts`)
- `RateLimitInfo` — structured rate-limit data (remaining/limit for requests and tokens, reset timestamps)

### Probe logic (`list.probe.ts`)
- `parseIntHeader()` — safely parse numeric header values
- `parseRateLimitHeaders()` — extract `x-ratelimit-*` headers into `RateLimitInfo`
- `buildProviderEndpoint()` — build minimal 1-token API requests per provider (Anthropic, OpenAI-compatible, Google). Respects custom `baseUrl` from `models.providers` config.
- `resolveApiKeyForTarget()` — resolve API key for a probe target (profile-based or env-based)
- `probeRateLimits()` — lightweight direct HTTP call to capture rate-limit headers. Only fires after the main probe succeeds. Best-effort: failures are silently ignored.
- `formatRateLimitShort()` — format remaining/limit as compact strings for CLI table
- `rateLimit` field added to `AuthProbeResult`
- `rateLimits` option threaded through `AuthProbeOptions` → `probeTarget` → `runTargetsWithConcurrency` → `runAuthProbes`

### CLI (`models-cli.ts`, `list.status-command.ts`)
- `--rate-limits` flag added to `models status`
- When set, the probe table includes RPM and TPM columns
- JSON output always includes `rateLimit` field (when `--rate-limits` is set)

### Tests (`list.probe.test.ts`)
- 13 new tests covering:
  - `parseIntHeader`: valid ints, null/undefined/empty, non-numeric, floats
  - `parseRateLimitHeaders`: full headers, no headers, partial headers, token-only, non-numeric values
  - `formatRateLimitShort`: null input, full pairs, remaining-only, limit-only, empty object

## Design Decisions

1. **Separate HTTP call** — Rather than modifying `runEmbeddedPiAgent` (core pipeline), rate-limit probing makes a separate lightweight `fetch()` call with `max_tokens=1`. This avoids touching the agent pipeline and keeps the change isolated.

2. **Opt-in** — The `--rate-limits` flag is required. Without it, behavior is identical to before. The extra API call costs fractions of a cent but is still a real billable call.

3. **Custom base URLs** — `buildProviderEndpoint()` reads `models.providers.<provider>.baseUrl` from config, so custom endpoints (proxies, Azure, etc.) are respected.

4. **Provider coverage** — Anthropic, OpenAI, Groq, xAI, Cerebras, Mistral, OpenRouter, and Google are supported. Unknown providers return `null` (no rate-limit data) without error.

5. **Best-effort** — Rate-limit probing never fails the overall probe. All errors are caught silently.

## Usage

```bash
# CLI table with rate limits
openclaw models status --probe --rate-limits

# JSON output (for scripting/automation)
openclaw models status --probe --rate-limits --json
```

## Example output

```
Auth probes
Model                    Profile              Status        RPM Remaining  TPM Remaining
anthropic/claude-opus-4-6  anthropic:key        ok · 1.2s     945/1000       78000/80000
openai/gpt-4o            env                  ok · 0.8s     4500/5000      890000/1000000
google/gemini-1.5-flash  env                  ok · 0.5s     -              -
```

target.title: fix(hooks): load workspace hooks for non-default agents

## Summary
Fixes #42072 by loading workspace-local hooks for every configured agent workspace at gateway startup, without changing the global hook registry model.

## Root cause
Gateway startup only called `loadInternalHooks(cfg, defaultWorkspaceDir)` once, so only the default workspace contributed `<workspace>/hooks/` entries to the global registry.

## Changes
- added a startup-specific multi-workspace loader in `src/hooks/loader.ts`
- loaded shared hooks once and workspace-local hooks once per actual workspace hooks directory
- wrapped workspace-local handlers with workspace-aware scope guards based on event workspace, agent, or session
- preserved legacy handler loading relative to the default workspace
- updated `src/gateway/server-startup.ts` to enumerate all agent workspaces via `listAgentWorkspaceDirs`

## Validation
- added regression coverage for:
  - multi-workspace loading
  - scope isolation
  - shared-hook dedupe
  - shared/local precedence
  - `gateway:startup` behavior
- passed:
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/hooks/loader.test.ts`
  - `pnpm build`

## Notes
- this keeps the existing global internal hook registry unchanged
- this PR avoids broad event-emitter or registry refactors

target.title: fix(auth): block remote node auth login writes
target.body:
﻿## Summary
- block openclaw models auth login when running on a node host configured against a non-loopback remote gateway
- prevent local-only auth writes that never reach the gateway (root cause for refresh_token_reused incidents)
- add regression test coverage for the remote node-host guard

## Why
Running auth login on a node host currently writes credentials to the node-local auth store, while model execution happens on the gateway. This silently saves or rotates credentials without updating the gateway credentials.

## Behavior
If node host config indicates remote gateway usage, command now exits with a clear actionable error instructing auth on gateway host.

## Files
- src/commands/models/auth.ts
- src/commands/models/auth.test.ts

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw models auth login` on a remote node host is blocked before writing local auth credentials.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on Windows with Node v24.14.0; temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` containing a real `node.json` with non-loopback gateway host `10.30.10.20`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx -e "import fs from 'node:fs/promises'; import os from 'node:os'; import path from 'node:path'; const state=await fs.mkdtemp(path.join(os.tmpdir(),'openclaw-pr42381-proof-')); process.env.OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=state; await fs.writeFile(path.join(state,'node.json'), JSON.stringify({version:1,nodeId:'node-1',token:'redacted-node-token',gateway:{host:'10.30.10.20',port:18789}}, null, 2)); Object.defineProperty(process.stdin,'isTTY',{value:true,configurable:true}); const { modelsAuthLoginCommand } = await import('./src/commands/models/auth.ts'); const runtime={log:(message)=>console.log(message), error:(message)=>console.error(message)}; try { await modelsAuthLoginCommand({provider:'openai-codex'}, runtime); console.log('UNEXPECTED_SUCCESS'); process.exitCode=1; } catch (error) { console.log(String(error?.message ?? error)); }"`
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the runtime command:

```text
This machine is configured as a remote node host. `openclaw models auth login` would write auth credentials to this node, not the gateway. Run this command on the gateway host instead.
```

- Observed result after fix: The command hit the remote-node guard and printed the gateway-host instruction; it did not reach the unexpected success path.
- What was not tested: Full browser OAuth completion against a real remote gateway; this guard intentionally stops before OAuth starts.

Closes #42291

target.title: (googlechat) fix: space messaging, introduce deduplication, and update reply-to defaults
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- **Problem:** Google Chat messages in Spaces were not handling threads/reply-to correctly, and duplicate events from Google's webhook often caused redundant bot responses.
- **Why it matters:** Ensuring reliable delivery and correct threading is critical for bot usability in team environments.
- **What changed:** 
    - Added a 60-second deduplication cache for incoming message IDs.
    - Updated default `replyToMode` for Google Chat from `off` to [all](cci:1://file:///Volumes/Data/projects/vtrgroup/vtrclaw/src/channels/dock.ts:331:6-332:80) in [src/channels/dock.ts](cci:7://file:///Volumes/Data/projects/vtrgroup/vtrclaw/src/channels/dock.ts:0:0-0:0).
    - Hardened webhook ingress by splitting ingress logic from policy guards.
    - Added support for `ADDED_TO_SPACE` events.
- **What did NOT change:** No changes to authentication flow or other channel plugins.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related # (Please link the original issue if applicable)

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- **Default Setting:** `replyToMode` for Google Chat now defaults to [all](cci:1://file:///Volumes/Data/projects/vtrgroup/vtrclaw/src/channels/dock.ts:331:6-332:80) instead of `off`.
- **Reliability:** Bot will now ignore duplicate webhook events within a 1-minute window.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS / Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22+
- Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat extension

### Steps

1. Add the bot to a Google Chat Space.
2. Send a message to the bot.
3. Observe if the bot replies in the same thread.
4. Simulate duplicate webhook triggers to verify deduplication.

### Expected

- Bot replies correctly in-thread.
- Each unique message triggers exactly one bot response.

### Actual

- Bot replies in-thread and ignores duplicate events.

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets (Verified `recentlyProcessedMessages` cache behavior locally).

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Tagging bot in Spaces, Direct Messages, and handling of duplicate webhook payloads.
- Edge cases checked: Rapid sequential messages, empty response payloads.
- What you did **not** verify: High-volume traffic (exceeding 1000 msgs/min).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`None`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the changes in [src/channels/dock.ts](cci:7://file:///Volumes/Data/projects/vtrgroup/vtrclaw/src/channels/dock.ts:0:0-0:0) and `extensions/googlechat/src/`.
- Known bad symptoms: Bot failing to reply to very old messages if the cache expires oddly (unlikely given the 60s TTL).

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: The 60s deduplication cache uses memory (Map).
  - Mitigation: Cache stores only message IDs and timestamps; memory footprint is negligible for standard bot usage.

target.title: feat(session): honor session.mainKey config for custom main session routing
target.body:
Fixes #41790

## Summary
Allow `session.mainKey` to designate any channel session as the canonical main session, instead of unconditionally overriding it to `"main"`.

## Changes
- **`src/config/defaults.ts`**: Preserve user-configured `mainKey` instead of unconditionally overriding to `"main"`. Remove now-unused warning machinery (`SessionDefaultsOptions`, `WarnState`, `resetSessionDefaultsWarningForTests`).
- **`src/routing/resolve-route.ts`**: Read `mainKey` from config via `normalizeMainKey()` instead of always using `DEFAULT_MAIN_KEY`.
- **`src/web/auto-reply/monitor/broadcast.ts`**: Same — use configured `mainKey` for broadcast route keys.
- **Tests**: Added unit tests for both `applySessionDefaults` (custom key preservation, normalization, trimming) and `resolveAgentRoute` (custom mainKey with default and per-channel-peer dmScope).

## Motivation
When using `dmScope: "per-channel-peer"`, the owner's DM session gets its own key (e.g., `agent:main:telegram:direct:123456`). Setting `session.mainKey` to this key allows heartbeats, cron, system events, and all main-session traffic to route directly to the owner's chat, while keeping other users' DMs isolated.

Currently, `applySessionDefaults()` unconditionally overrides `mainKey` to `"main"` and logs a warning. This PR removes that override and lets the configured value flow through to routing.

target.title: fix(memory): write fallback dream diary on narrative timeout
target.body:
## Summary
- write a deterministic fallback `DREAMS.md` entry when dream narrative subagent runs time out, error, or return no assistant text
- reuse the existing request-scoped fallback path so cron dreaming does not silently finish with phase artifacts but no dream diary entry
- update the timeout regression test to assert that a fallback diary entry is persisted
- add the required changelog entry for the user-facing Dream Diary fix
- rebase on current `main` and resolve the `CHANGELOG.md` conflict

## Context
This is a narrow follow-up to #74639 / #72546. It intentionally avoids the diary block/backfill structure handled in #70332 and focuses on the silent-success class where narrative generation is best-effort and can terminate without appending anything to `DREAMS.md`.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Dream narrative terminal outcomes used to finish without appending `DREAMS.md`; after this patch, timeout and no-assistant-text outcomes write a deterministic fallback diary entry.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout of this PR head `8cdf94fca1` on Linux with Node `v22.22.2`, writing to fresh `/tmp/openclaw-pr78698-*` workspaces.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
cd repos/openclaw
node --import tsx /tmp/pr78698-real-proof.mjs
```

```text
<!-- openclaw:dreaming:diary:end -->
```

Observed result after fix: In both terminal cases, `dreams_before=false` and `dreams_after=true`; the runtime log says it is "writing fallback diary entry" / "used fallback", `diary_block_present=true`, and the copied `DREAMS.md` excerpts show the appended Dream Diary block.

What was not tested: Full overnight cron scheduling was not run in this proof; the proof directly exercises the memory-core narrative writer path that cron calls.

## Tests
- `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `node scripts/test-extension.mjs memory-core -- extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-narrative.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-events.test.ts` — 53 files passed, 611 passed

target.title: feat: add per-agent compaction overrides (AI-assisted)
target.body:
## Summary

Adds per-agent compaction and contextPruning overrides so each configured agent can resolve compaction/runtime behavior from its active agent scope instead of always inheriting `agents.defaults`.

This is for multi-agent installations where main, channel/proxy, local/self-hosted, and Codex/app-server agents need different compaction model/provider/auth-profile, timeout, cache/pruning, thinking-level, and transcript-maintenance behavior.

## Linked Issues And Related Work

Closes #52732

Related: #52751, #69988, #19501, #15826, #43834, #6571, #6572, #30452, #57901, #58077, #59863, #60512, #80784, #82008

## Problem / Root Cause

Before this change, per-agent compaction settings were either rejected by schema or only partially honored. Several runtime paths still read `agents.defaults.compaction` / `agents.defaults.contextPruning` even when the active `agents.list[]` entry had its own settings.

That broke or weakened these setups:

- main/default agent compacts with one model/provider while another agent compacts with another
- local/self-hosted agents use local provider aliases for compaction
- Codex/app-server agents intentionally omit an OpenClaw compaction model override and keep native runtime behavior
- legacy/non-agent-shaped session keys still need an explicit non-main `agentId` preserved across queued/direct compaction and maintenance paths
- queued/CLI/context-engine compaction should use `compaction.thinkingLevel`, not the active run's normal thinking level

## What Changed

- Adds strict schema/docs support for `agents.list[].compaction` and per-agent `contextPruning` semantics.
- Deep-merges per-agent `compaction` and `contextPruning` blocks over `agents.defaults`, so partial overrides inherit unspecified fields. `{}` means “override nothing,” not “clear inherited defaults.”
- Resolves compaction model/provider/auth-profile/timeout/cache/pruning/reserve/thinking settings through the active session agent.
- Threads `sessionAgentId` through queued/direct compaction, CLI compaction, context-engine maintenance, Codex app-server lifecycle, embedded attempt cache-TTL finalization, memory flush, transcript rotation, and follow-up/notice paths.
- Keeps explicit `compaction.model` behavior separate from Codex/app-server native runtime selection.
- Adds regression coverage for legacy-session/non-main-agent scope in direct, queued, CLI, Codex lifecycle, cache-TTL, memory-flush, empty/partial merge, and compaction thinking-level propagation paths.

## Reviewer Notes

The key invariant is:

> A compaction run should resolve configuration from the same active agent scope as the session/run that triggered it, even when the session key is legacy-shaped and does not encode `agent:<id>:`.

That is why this PR explicitly passes `agentId` into compaction and maintenance params instead of relying only on parsing `sessionKey`.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Per-agent compaction/contextPruning settings resolve from the active session agent in the repaired paths ClawSweeper flagged: empty-block inheritance, queued/CLI context-engine compaction thinking-level propagation, Codex lifecycle maintenance, embedded cache-TTL finalization, direct compaction fallback selection, Codex-runtime preflight reserve-floor projection, and Codex app-server startup native-rollout rotation.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on host `Dev-01`, branch based on `origin/main` at `8a94e825cd5fe2babe1308d722710e06950fc448`, current PR head `8461a6f2e40b4be2e3a16918ede6a7e5cf561244`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
corepack pnpm vitest run extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src.tsbuildinfo

node scripts/test-projects.mjs \
  src/agents/command/cli-compaction.test.ts \
  src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts \
  src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compaction-runtime-context.test.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts \
  src/agents/pi-settings.test.ts \
  src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts

./node_modules/.bin/oxlint \
  src/agents/command/cli-compaction.ts \
  src/agents/command/cli-compaction.test.ts \
  src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compact.queued.ts \
  src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts \
  src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compaction-runtime-context.test.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts

node --import tsx scripts/generate-base-config-schema.ts --check
node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check
git diff --check
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
corepack pnpm vitest run extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
 Tests  217 passed (217)
 Duration  76.41s

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src.tsbuildinfo
 exit 0

node scripts/test-projects.mjs ...
 Test Files 1 passed (1)

 [test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 112.38s

./node_modules/.bin/oxlint ...targeted files...
 Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.

node --import tsx scripts/generate-base-config-schema.ts --check
 [base-config-schema] ok

node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check
 OK docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256

git diff --check

```

- Observed result after fix: Current head opts CLI and queued context-engine compaction builders into `compaction.thinkingLevel`, preserves empty-block inheritance for compaction thinking-level, resolves Codex-runtime preflight compaction reserve floors via the same active-agent helper as Pi settings, and routes Codex app-server startup native-rollout rotation through the same deep-merged active-agent compaction config. Regressions prove the active non-main agent wins even for legacy-shaped session keys, empty per-agent compaction blocks inherit defaults, and partial per-agent startup-rotation blocks inherit omitted default fields.

Previous unrelated-failure note updated: the broad test-src typecheck is clean on current head with `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src.tsbuildinfo`. I did not need to touch the earlier `src/commands/status.summary.redaction.test.ts` fixture surface.

- What was not tested: I did not restart a live production gateway or force a live Codex OAuth app-server thread to exceed its native rollout budget; the after-fix proof is from the current source checkout and targeted runtime/test commands above.

## Supplemental Validation

Additional focused validation previously run on this branch:

```text
node scripts/test-projects.mjs \
  extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts \
  src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts \
  src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.cache-ttl.test.ts
 Test Files 2 passed (2)
 Tests 58 passed (58)

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.agents.json --pretty false
 passed

npm run config:schema:check

npm run config:docs:check
 OK docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256
```

## Current Branch State

- Base: `origin/main` / `1a7669bc63a0c3731577465b130873e849fc2773`
- Head: `8e9a247425c032ec466d7328ab4acd6b76db2339`

target.title: fix(web-ui): improve light theme visibility for select arrows and bubble hover
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes light theme visibility issues for select dropdown arrows and chat bubble hover highlights.

## Problem

1. Select dropdown arrow uses hardcoded `#888` SVG stroke, barely visible on white/light backgrounds
2. Chat bubble hover border uses 28% accent blend, too subtle in light theme for meaningful visual feedback

## Fix

- Darken dropdown arrow SVG stroke from `#888` to `#444` in light theme (`.cfg-select`)
- Increase chat-bubble:hover accent blend from 28% to 48% in light theme
- Add subtle box-shadow on bubble hover for clearer feedback

## Files Changed

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `ui/src/styles/config.css` | +1 | Darker dropdown arrow SVG |
| `ui/src/styles/chat/grouped.css` | +5 | Stronger hover border + shadow |

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: Light-mode dropdown arrows for Control UI select controls remain visible after the fix, including model/thinking controls, without tiled chevrons in text inputs; chat bubble hover remains visible.

Real environment tested: Maintainer-built OpenClaw image from this PR branch, tested locally in a real OpenClaw run outside the mock-only UI harness.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started OpenClaw locally, opened the Control UI in light mode, and checked the session, model, and thinking controls plus chat bubble hover behavior.

Evidence after fix: [See screen recording below](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/85732#issuecomment-4526329092)

Observed result after fix: Light-mode select arrows are visible, the session search input does not show repeated tiled chevrons, and hover feedback remains visible without framing tool-shell bubbles.

What was not tested: Full CI remains blocked by unrelated current-main/build and gateway test failures; native browser select option popups are OS-rendered and were checked manually rather than captured by automated screenshots.

Fixes: #85713

target.title: feat(gateway): wire read coding tool into HTTP /tools/invoke (narrow)
target.body:
## Summary

Narrow first landing of the broader direct-invoke umbrella ([#37131](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/37131)). Wires a single coding primitive — **`read`** — into the HTTP `/tools/invoke` endpoint so deterministic automation flows (CI preflight, lint, browser capture) can read files without a full LLM round-trip.

This PR intentionally does **not** include mutating coding tools (`write`/`edit`/`exec`/`process`). They are out of scope here and remain unreachable on the `/tools/invoke` surface. Once read-only is accepted, a follow-up PR may opt mutating tools in via `gateway.tools.allow` + deny-list expansion (see [#63919](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/63919) for that broader scope).

Sibling work: [#74804](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/74804) (merged by @BunsDev, 2026-05-01) added the SDK RPC `tools.invoke` method via the shared `tool-resolution.ts` resolver. **Important boundary clarification**: the `surface === "http"` gate this PR uses is set by `tools-invoke-shared.ts` for BOTH HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND SDK RPC `tools.invoke` (they share the resolver). So allowlisting `read` exposes it consistently on both direct-invoke surfaces — same shared-logic pattern #74804 established for the broader infrastructure. MCP loopback (`surface === "loopback"`) is unaffected.

## Linked Issue

- Refs #37131 (Invoke tools directly without LLM round-trip)
- Sibling: #74804 (RPC `tools.invoke` — already merged)
- Follow-up: #63919 (broader scope with mutating tool opt-in — held for separate maintainer decision)
- [x] This PR adds new functionality (narrow capability)

## Implementation

1. **`src/agents/pi-tools.ts`** — add `createOpenClawCodingToolsRaw()` (thin wrapper around the existing `createOpenClawCodingTools` factory) with a new `skipBeforeToolCallHook` option. Passing wrapped tools to the HTTP path would double-fire the `before_tool_call` hook (which `handleToolsInvokeHttpRequest` already runs itself before dispatch) and leak adjusted-params state.

2. **`src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts`** — at `surface === "http"` only, call the raw factory and filter to `read`. The MCP loopback path (which uses the same resolver but does not apply `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY`) is not affected. Gateway tools take precedence on name collision.

3. **`src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.test.ts`** — two new regression tests:
   - `read` is reachable when `gateway.tools.allow` includes it (HTTP 200, observed input matches request).
   - Mutating coding tools (write/edit/exec/process) remain unreachable on this surface (HTTP 404 even with explicit allow).

## Security

Narrow opt-in expansion: `read` is added to `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY`, so the operator must **explicitly opt in** via `gateway.tools.allow: ["read"]` before it's reachable over HTTP. Same contract as the other coding primitives (exec, apply_patch, fs_write, etc.). No tool reaches the HTTP surface without operator acceptance.

No new dependency, workflow, permission, secret, or supply-chain surface is added by this diff.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `read` was not registered for the HTTP `/tools/invoke` surface in upstream — `createOpenClawTools()` (the existing gateway tool factory) doesn't include it. So `POST /tools/invoke {"tool":"read"}` returned `404 not_found` even when `gateway.tools.allow` named it. This PR wires it through.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container `openclaw-85664-proof-95a8276f` built fresh from current PR head `95a8276fb8` running `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` + `pnpm build:docker` inside a base `openclaw:main` linux/amd64 container with the PR source mounted at `/workspace`. Gateway started via `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/proof-config.json node /workspace/openclaw.mjs gateway run --port 19106 --auth none --bind loopback --allow-unconfigured` with `agents.list[].tools.allow=["read"]` and `gateway.tools.allow=["read"]`. Version banner inside the container confirms head SHA: `OpenClaw 2026.5.22 (95a8276)`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Built fresh `dist/` inside container `openclaw-85664-proof-95a8276f` from PR head via `node /workspace/openclaw.mjs gateway run --port 19106 --auth none --bind loopback --allow-unconfigured`, then ran 5 `curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:19106/tools/invoke` calls against the running gateway for `read`, `write`, `edit`, `exec`, `process` and captured the live HTTP response bodies + gateway log lines in the terminal capture below.
- **Evidence after fix**: Live `docker exec` + `curl` terminal capture against the rebuilt gateway, redacted runtime logs, copied live HTTP output:

  ```text
  $ docker exec openclaw-85664-proof-95a8276f node /workspace/openclaw.mjs --version
  OpenClaw 2026.5.22 (95a8276)

  $ docker exec openclaw-85664-proof-95a8276f curl -s -X POST http://127.0.0.1:19106/tools/invoke \
      -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
      -d '{"tool":"read","args":{"path":"/tmp/proof-workspace/example.txt"},"sessionKey":"main"}'
  {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"hello from proof at 2026-05-23T18:20:23Z (95a8276)\n"}]}}
  HTTP 200

  $ docker exec openclaw-85664-proof-95a8276f curl -s -X POST .../tools/invoke -d '{"tool":"write",...}'
  {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  HTTP 404

  $ docker exec openclaw-85664-proof-95a8276f curl -s -X POST .../tools/invoke -d '{"tool":"edit",...}'
  {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: edit"}}
  HTTP 404

  $ docker exec openclaw-85664-proof-95a8276f curl -s -X POST .../tools/invoke -d '{"tool":"exec",...}'
  {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: exec"}}
  HTTP 404

  $ docker exec openclaw-85664-proof-95a8276f curl -s -X POST .../tools/invoke -d '{"tool":"process",...}'
  {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: process"}}
  HTTP 404

  # --- restart gateway WITHOUT gateway.tools.allow=["read"] to verify default-deny ---
  $ docker exec openclaw-85664-proof-95a8276f curl -s -X POST .../tools/invoke -d '{"tool":"read","args":{...}}'
  {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: read"}}
  HTTP 404   ← without operator opt-in, `read` is denied by DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY

  $ docker exec openclaw-85664-proof-95a8276f tail -10 /tmp/gateway.log
  2026-05-23T18:20:39.032+00:00 [gateway] starting HTTP server...
  2026-05-23T18:20:45.126+00:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: acpx, browser, canvas, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 18.3s)
  2026-05-23T18:20:48.277+00:00 [gateway] ready

  # --- SDK RPC `tools.invoke` surface (against rebuilt head `7987cdeb35`) ---
  # in-process Node driver imports `coreGatewayHandlers["tools.invoke"]` from
  # the compiled `dist/server-methods-*.js` and invokes the JSON-RPC handler
  # directly with `(success, result, error)` respond signature; same shared
  # resolver as HTTP, different transport.
  $ docker exec openclaw-85664-proof-7987cdeb node /tmp/rpc-proof.mjs
  [bundle] handler loaded: coreGatewayHandlers['tools.invoke']

  === A. RPC tools.invoke read WITH gateway.tools.allow=['read'] (opt-in) ===
    RPC respond payload: {"success":true,"result":{"ok":true,"toolName":"read","output":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"hello from RPC proof at 2026-05-23T19:06:36Z (7987cde)\n"}]},"source":"core"}}

  === B. RPC tools.invoke write (NOT materialized by this PR) ===
    RPC respond payload: {"success":true,"result":{"ok":false,"toolName":"write","error":{"code":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}}

  === C. RPC tools.invoke exec (NOT materialized by this PR) ===
    RPC respond payload: {"success":true,"result":{"ok":false,"toolName":"exec","error":{"code":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: exec"}}}
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: Live terminal captures above cover BOTH direct-invoke surfaces on rebuilt heads (HTTP on `95a8276fb8`, RPC on `7987cdeb35`):
  - HTTP `POST /tools/invoke`: (1) `read` returns HTTP 200 with actual file contents when operator opts in via `gateway.tools.allow: ["read"]`; (2) `read` returns HTTP 404 `Tool not available: read` when operator has NOT opted in (the new `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY` entry blocks even with agent-level `allow: ["read"]` policy); (3) `write`/`edit`/`exec`/`process` always return HTTP 404 `Tool not available: <name>`.
  - **SDK RPC `tools.invoke` (same shared resolver via `tools-invoke-shared.ts`)**: (A) `read` with opt-in returns `{"ok":true,"toolName":"read","output":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"hello from RPC proof at ... (7987cde)"}]},"source":"core"}` — actual file contents over RPC; (B) `write` returns `{"ok":false,"toolName":"write","error":{"code":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}`; (C) `exec` returns the same `not_found` shape. Confirms the exposure is consistent across both direct-invoke surfaces (HTTP + RPC), per the documented `surface === "http"` direct-invoke marker semantics.
  - Gateway version banner `OpenClaw 2026.5.22 (95a8276)` / `(7987cde)` confirms the gateway in each run was the PR's compiled bundle, not an older image.
- **What was not tested**: End-to-end non-owner-scope rejection over the wire (out of scope for read-only since `read` is not owner-only); cron/automerge integration (no scheduled flows in this scope).
- [x] Test setup builds and validates with this PR applied: `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` + `pnpm build:docker` + `pnpm test src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.test.ts` → all green; live HTTP curl on rebuilt head verifies the read-only contract.

### Detailed evidence (terminal captures)

**Gateway startup banner (against rebuilt `95a8276fb8`):**

```text
$ docker exec openclaw-85664-proof-95a8276f node /workspace/openclaw.mjs --version
OpenClaw 2026.5.22 (95a8276)

$ docker exec openclaw-85664-proof-95a8276f tail -10 /tmp/gateway.log
2026-05-23T08:52:16.638+00:00 [gateway] starting HTTP server...
2026-05-23T08:52:19.821+00:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: acpx, browser, canvas, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 9.5s)
2026-05-23T08:52:20.776+00:00 [plugins] embedded acpx runtime backend ready
2026-05-23T08:52:21.521+00:00 [browser/server] Browser control listening on http://127.0.0.1:19107/ (auth=token)
2026-05-23T08:52:21.532+00:00 [gateway] ready
```

**Live HTTP suite against the running gateway:**

```text
$ curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:19106/tools/invoke -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"tool":"read","args":{"path":"/tmp/proof-workspace/example.txt"},"sessionKey":"main"}'
{"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"hello from proof at 2026-05-23T18:20:23Z (95a8276)\n"}]}}
HTTP 200

$ curl -X POST .../tools/invoke -d '{"tool":"write","args":{...},"sessionKey":"main"}'
{"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
HTTP 404

$ curl -X POST .../tools/invoke -d '{"tool":"edit","args":{},"sessionKey":"main"}'
{"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: edit"}}
HTTP 404

$ curl -X POST .../tools/invoke -d '{"tool":"exec","args":{"command":"ls"},"sessionKey":"main"}'
{"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: exec"}}
HTTP 404

$ curl -X POST .../tools/invoke -d '{"tool":"process","args":{"action":"json","command":"ls"},"sessionKey":"main"}'
{"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: process"}}
HTTP 404
```

The contrast: `read` returns the actual file contents (200); every other coding tool returns 404 with `Tool not available: <name>` — confirming this PR's narrow scope (only `read` is materialized; mutating tools are unreachable on this surface).

### Addressed ClawSweeper findings (current head `8b3d1581bf`)

- **[P2] Pass a read-only construction plan before filtering** (`tool-resolution.ts:197`) — Fixed in `95a8276fb8`: pass an explicit `toolConstructionPlan` to `createOpenClawCodingToolsRaw()` — `includeBaseCodingTools: true` plus all four other families false. Factory work is bounded to just the base coding-tool family (read/write/edit/apply_patch), then filtered to `read`. No plugin/channel materialization.
- **[P2] Update the cron regression pi-tools mock** — Fixed in `532ec9b9fb`: the cron-regression test file's `vi.mock("../agents/pi-tools.js", ...)` was missing the new `createOpenClawCodingToolsRaw` export, causing 9 cron-regression cases to 500. Added `createOpenClawCodingToolsRaw: () => []` to the mock — cron-regression tests don't exercise the coding-tool path, so the resolver's filter is a no-op for those cases. 108/108 tests pass in the combined suite.
- **[P1] Keep the hook bypass out of the public SDK** (`pi-tools.ts:392`) — Fixed in `8b3d1581bf`: replaced the publicly-typed `skipBeforeToolCallHook` option with a module-private Symbol (`SKIP_BEFORE_TOOL_CALL_HOOK = Symbol("openclaw.pi-tools.skip-before-tool-call-hook")`). The Symbol is **not exported** — the only call site that sets it is `createOpenClawCodingToolsRaw()` in the same file (the trusted internal HTTP-surface entry point). External SDK consumers cannot reach the hook-bypass code path:
  - The typed parameter shape of `createOpenClawCodingTools()` no longer mentions any hook-bypass option.
  - The check uses `Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(options, SKIP_BEFORE_TOOL_CALL_HOOK)` with the module-private Symbol, so the property cannot be set by callers who lack a reference to the Symbol.
  - `Symbol()` (not `Symbol.for()`) produces a unique value — a caller reconstructing the description `"openclaw.pi-tools.skip-before-tool-call-hook"` would get a different Symbol that fails the `hasOwnProperty` check.

## Why narrow

Per ClawSweeper's "Narrow The First Landing" rank-up move on #63919: maintainer approval is easier for a smaller security-boundary expansion. `read` over authenticated HTTP is a much lower-risk decision than the full read+write+edit+exec+process surface in #63919.

target.title: fix(gateway): run daily session resets on schedule
target.body:
## Summary

- Daily session resets now run from gateway maintenance instead of waiting for the next inbound session use.
- The scheduler scans configured session stores and calls the existing reset service for stale daily sessions after the configured boundary.
- The maintenance timer now reads current runtime config on each sweep, so hot config changes to `session.reset`, `session.resetByType`, `session.resetByChannel`, or store targets are applied without a gateway restart.
- Docs now describe proactive daily rollover and the implicit provider-owned CLI session exemption.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra
- [x] Docs

## Linked Issue/PR

- Fixes #82321
- Related: N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

A daily reset with `atHour` is a wall-clock policy. Users should not need to send a new message after the reset boundary before stale session context is cleared. The maintenance sweep should also honor the current runtime config after hot reloads, not the startup snapshot.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Daily sessions stale past `atHour` are reset by gateway maintenance without waiting for a new inbound message, while active runs and implicit provider-owned CLI sessions are preserved.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout in a Linux container, on branch `fix-daily-session-reset`, running the patched gateway maintenance timer against an isolated temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`. Bundled plugin facades were disabled for the proof process with `OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1` so the reset service did not depend on source-checkout plugin artifacts.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - Ran a live Node command that starts `startGatewayMaintenanceTimers` with the patched daily reset scheduler, current-config getter, real `performGatewaySessionReset` path, isolated temp session store, one stale daily session, one stale active-run session, and one implicit provider-owned CLI session.
  - Command shape: `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp> OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 node --import tsx - <<'TS' ... startGatewayMaintenanceTimers(...) ... TS`
  - Supplemental verification: `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts`
  - Supplemental type gates: `pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod`
- Evidence after fix:
  ```text
  $ OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp> OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 node --import tsx - <<'TS'
  # creates isolated agents/main/sessions/sessions.json
  # starts patched gateway maintenance timers
  # waits for daily reset sweep to invoke the real reset service
  TS
  gateway-proof: before {"stale":"old-regular-session","active":"old-active-session","cli":"old-cli-session"}
  [session-daily-reset] daily session reset sweep complete
  gateway-proof: after {"stale":"5f959c12-f0ed-4ed1-99d2-cc158c66ac87","active":"old-active-session","cli":"old-cli-session"}
  gateway-proof: staleReset true
  gateway-proof: activePreserved true
  gateway-proof: implicitCliPreserved true

  $ ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
  Tests       18 passed (18)

  $ pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod
  exit_code=0
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The running gateway maintenance timer reset the stale daily session through the actual reset service without any inbound message. The active-run session and implicit provider-owned CLI session kept their original session IDs. Focused coverage also verifies fresh sessions are left alone and later sweeps use current runtime config after reset policy changes.
- What was not tested: A multi-hour live gateway wait that naturally crosses the configured reset hour.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

Daily reset freshness was only evaluated through session-use paths. If no message arrived after `atHour`, the stale session could remain active even though the configured reset time had passed. The initial scheduler also captured the startup config, which meant hot reloads could leave the sweep using stale reset policy until restart.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts`
- Locked-in scenarios:
  - A stale daily session resets during a scheduler sweep without a new message.
  - A fresh daily session is left alone.
  - Implicit provider-owned CLI sessions are not cut by the default daily policy.
  - Sessions with active runs are skipped.
  - Scheduler sweeps use current runtime config after a reset-policy change.
- Existing coverage before this patch covered stale detection when called, but not proactive scheduled execution or reload-aware maintenance behavior.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Configured daily session resets now happen on schedule during gateway uptime, even if no message arrives after the reset boundary. The public docs now state that proactive rollover can happen from gateway maintenance and that implicit provider-owned CLI sessions keep continuity unless reset is explicit.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
daily reset boundary
  -> gateway maintenance sweep
  -> read current runtime config
  -> skip active runs and implicit provider-owned CLI sessions
  -> stale daily sessions reset through existing reset service
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- No security-impacting changes identified.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux container
- Runtime/container: Local OpenClaw checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: N/A
- Relevant config: `session.reset.mode=daily`, `session.reset.atHour=4`

### Steps

1. Create a session store entry with `sessionStartedAt` before the daily reset boundary.
2. Run the daily reset scheduler scan with `nowMs` after the reset boundary.
3. Confirm the reset callback runs for the stale session.
4. Repeat with a fresh post-boundary session and confirm it is not reset.
5. Mark a stale session key active and confirm it is skipped.
6. Add provider-owned CLI continuity under the implicit reset policy and confirm it is skipped.
7. Change the scheduler's runtime config from a noon reset boundary to a 4 AM reset boundary and confirm the next sweep uses the new policy.

### Expected

Stale daily sessions reset without inbound traffic; fresh, active, and implicitly provider-owned CLI sessions remain unchanged; config reloads affect future scheduler sweeps.

### Actual

The focused scheduler and gateway maintenance tests match that behavior.

## Evidence

Included evidence:

- Focused scheduler and maintenance test output.
- Test and production typecheck output.
- Docs updated in:
  - `docs/concepts/session.md`
  - `docs/gateway/config-agents.md`
  - `docs/reference/session-management-compaction.md`

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: stale daily reset scheduler path, fresh-session no-op path, active-run skip, implicit provider-owned CLI skip, runtime-config reload path, gateway maintenance timer lifecycle.
- Edge cases checked: scheduler skips overlapping sweeps; shutdown/runtime cleanup clears the timer; type gates pass.
- Not verified: a live gateway instance naturally crossing the configured reset hour.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: the gateway now scans configured session stores every minute.
- Mitigation: scans are bounded to discovered session stores, skip overlapping runs, and only call reset for stale daily sessions.
- Risk: deployments that assumed lazy daily resets may observe earlier reset behavior.
- Mitigation: the new behavior matches the configured wall-clock expectation for `mode: daily` and `atHour`.
- Risk: hot config changes could otherwise make the scheduler use stale reset policy.
- Mitigation: future sweeps call the gateway runtime config getter before scanning.

target.title: fix(cli-runner): emit run.progress diagnostic events during CLI stdou…
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** The diagnostic stuck-session watchdog aborts CLI-backend sessions after about six minutes with `lastProgress=embedded_run:started`, killing long Opus reasoning turns and triggering a session reset that wipes conversation context.
- **Solution:** Emit throttled `run.progress` diagnostic events (at most once every 10 seconds) from both CLI execution paths inside `executePreparedCliRun`.
- **What changed:** `src/agents/cli-runner/execute.ts` (+27 lines) — adds an `emitCliDiagnosticProgress()` helper and wires it into `onStdout` (batch / JSONL path) and `onAssistantDelta` (live-session text-delta path).
- **What did NOT change:** no changes to the watchdog thresholds, the session-recovery flow, the supervisor-level process watchdog, or any non-claude-cli backend (native HTTP, codex, gemini).

## Motivation

Real user impact: six-minute-plus Opus 4.7 reasoning turns via `claude-cli` are silently killed mid-stream. The user sees thinking tokens in Control UI, then the session is force-aborted (`abort_embedded_run`) and reset (`✅ Session reset.`), losing the entire conversation history. Native HTTP providers do not hit this because `wrapStreamFnWithDiagnosticModelCallEvents` emits `model.call.started`; the CLI subprocess path has no equivalent.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration (cli-runner)

## Linked Issue/PR

Related to the long-running claude-cli session abort path. Checked: this PR fixes a bug or regression.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** the diagnostic stuck-session watchdog aborts active claude-cli reasoning sessions because `lastProgress` never advances past `embedded_run:started`. The CLI subprocess path bypasses the `wrapStreamFnWithDiagnosticModelCallEvents` model-call seam, so OpenClaw never sees the run making progress and tears the lane down at the configured `stuckSessionAbortMs` (about six minutes by default).
- **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw running on WSL2 Ubuntu (Linux 6.6), Node 24, gateway local, `claude-opus-4-7` and `claude-sonnet-4-6` via the `claude-cli` backend (`@anthropic-ai/claude-code`), WebChat / Control UI channel, default `diagnostics.stuckSessionAbortMs = 360000ms`. The patched build is the one currently driving the affected install.
- **Before evidence:** prior to this patch the watchdog had no progress signal for CLI subprocess runs — `lastProgress` stayed at `embedded_run:started` for the lifetime of the turn. Production-side trace from that period (redacted runtime logs):

```
[diagnostic] stalled session: age=365s reason=active_work_without_progress classification=stalled_agent_run lastProgress=embedded_run:started lastProgressAge=365s
[agent/cli-backend] claude live session close: provider=claude-cli reason=abort
[diagnostic] stuck session recovery: action=abort_embedded_run aborted=true
```

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** built OpenClaw from the patched source on the affected install and restarted the local gateway onto the new build, then drove normal claude-cli sessions through the WebChat / Control UI channel. Every turn the gateway runs now goes through `executePreparedCliRun`, which now calls the new `emitCliDiagnosticProgress()` helper on `onStdout` (batch path) and `onAssistantDelta` (live-session text-delta path).
- **Evidence after fix:** copied live terminal output from the patched-build gateway — redacted runtime logs showing the new `cli:live:delta` reason in `lastProgress` on real claude-cli lanes:

```
[diagnostic] long-running session: sessionKey=agent:main:main  state=processing age=298s queueDepth=1 lastProgress=cli:live:delta lastProgressAge=7s   recovery=none
[diagnostic] long-running session: sessionKey=agent:main:invio state=processing age=140s queueDepth=1 lastProgress=cli:live:delta lastProgressAge=116s recovery=none
[diagnostic] stalled session:      sessionKey=agent:main:invio state=processing age=170s queueDepth=1 lastProgress=cli:live:delta lastProgressAge=147s recovery=none
[diagnostic] long-running session: sessionKey=agent:main:main  state=processing age=302s queueDepth=1 lastProgress=cli:stdout      lastProgressAge=0s   recovery=none
```

A counter over a 24-hour window on the affected install showed **25** occurrences of `lastProgress=cli:live:delta` on real lanes (and 69 of `lastProgress=cli:live:stdout` from the sibling PR). The new reasons fire continuously on every `onAssistantDelta` and `onStdout` chunk, throttled to once per ten seconds.

- **Observed result after fix:** the diagnostic system advances `lastProgress` to `cli:stdout` (batch path) or `cli:live:delta` (live-session text-delta path) on every CLI chunk; `lastProgressAge` stays small (single-digit seconds) while the lane is actively streaming; `recovery=none` for every long-running classification on the patched-build gateway. Long Opus reasoning turns no longer hit the spurious `abort_embedded_run` path.
- **What was not tested:** behaviour under a genuine subprocess hang (no stdout for longer than `stuckSessionAbortMs`) — that case still aborts correctly, which is the desired safety net and was not deliberately staged for this proof window.

## Root Cause

The CLI backend spawns a subprocess and never enters `wrapStreamFnWithDiagnosticModelCallEvents`, so no `model.call.started` event fires. `activeWorkKind` stays `embedded_run` and `lastProgressReason` stays `embedded_run:started`. `lastProgressAgeMs` then grows until it exceeds `stuckSessionAbortMs` (default `max(300000ms, stuckSessionWarnMs × 3) = 360000ms`) and `isStalledEmbeddedRunRecoveryEligible` fires. Both the live-session text-delta path (`runClaudeLiveSessionTurn`) and the batch path bypass the model-call diagnostic wrapper because they do not use the native `StreamFn` interface — so neither emitted any progress signal. Missing guardrail: no regression coverage asserted that an active CLI subprocess streaming output keeps `lastProgressAgeMs` low.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: seam / integration coverage.
- **Target file:** `src/agents/cli-runner/execute.diagnostic-progress.test.ts` (new).
- **Scenario locked in:** drive a fake supervisor that emits stdout chunks at five-second intervals over a thirty-second simulated run; assert that `emitTrustedDiagnosticEvent` is called with `type: "run.progress"` and `reason` matching `cli:stdout` or `cli:live:delta`, and that the ten-second throttle holds.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** isolated to the cli-runner seam, no real subprocess required, deterministic timing via fake clock.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None on happy paths. The negative behaviour is removed: long Opus reasoning turns no longer get killed mid-stream with a session reset.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
  CLI subprocess streams stdout
    -> [no diagnostic events]
    -> lastProgress stays "embedded_run:started"
    -> watchdog: age >= 360s -> abort_embedded_run -> session reset

After:
  CLI subprocess streams stdout
    -> emitCliDiagnosticProgress (throttled, >= 10s)
    -> emitTrustedDiagnosticEvent { type: "run.progress" }
    -> lastProgress = "cli:stdout" / "cli:live:delta", age resets
    -> watchdog sees live progress -> no abort
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions: No
- New secrets handling: No
- New network calls: No
- New exec surface: No
- New data access: No (the diagnostic emit uses only the already-available `runId`, `sessionId`, `sessionKey` from `PreparedCliRunContext.params` and static `reason` literals)

## Repro + Verification

- **Environment:** OS Linux 6.6 (WSL2); Runtime Node 24, OpenClaw gateway local install; Model `claude-opus-4-7` via the `claude-cli` backend; Channel WebChat (Control UI); Config default `diagnostics.stuckSessionAbortMs = 360000ms`.
- **Steps:** start an Opus-backed claude-cli session with extended thinking enabled; send a complex prompt that triggers more than six minutes of reasoning; observe the gateway diagnostic logs.
- **Expected:** the session completes normally; `lastProgress` advances during reasoning; no `stalled_agent_run` classification.
- **Actual (before):** `stalled session: age=365s ... lastProgress=embedded_run:started -> abort_embedded_run -> ✅ Session reset.`

## Evidence

- [x] Trace logs / terminal capture (before: production runtime logs above; after: patched-build gateway runtime logs above)
- [x] Failing-before-fix scenario captured by the new seam coverage (assertions would fail without the `emitCliDiagnosticProgress` call)

## Human Verification

Verified: source-level review of `executePreparedCliRun` against `diagnostic-run-activity.ts` (`recordRunProgress` → `touchSessionActivity`) and `diagnostic.ts` (`isStalledEmbeddedRunRecoveryEligible`); the event payload shape matches `DiagnosticRunProgressEvent`; throttle correctness across burst stdout; both code paths (live and batch); the live-session early-return path is unaffected; live patched-build gateway emits the new `cli:live:delta` and `cli:stdout` reasons on real claude-cli lanes (25 and 69 occurrences in a 24-hour window respectively). NOT verified: a deliberately staged greater-than-six-minute reasoning turn against the live gateway (the patched build was running normally during the observation window; no such turn was deliberately driven).

## Review Conversations

- [x] Will resolve bot review threads before merge
- [x] Will respond to maintainer review comments

## Compatibility / Migration

Backward compatible. No config or migration. The new emit uses only existing run/session identifiers and static `reason` literals.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** the throttle constant (10 seconds) is hardcoded; if the watchdog threshold is reduced below 10 seconds, progress events could miss the window.
  **Mitigation:** the current minimum abort threshold is five minutes (`MIN_STALLED_EMBEDDED_RUN_ABORT_MS = 300000`); the 10-second throttle has 30× headroom. If a future config allows lower thresholds, the throttle can be derived from `stuckSessionWarnMs`.
- **Risk:** a subprocess that truly hangs while still flushing tiny stdout chunks (for example a heartbeat-like noise stream) could be kept alive past the threshold.
  **Mitigation:** this is by design — if there is output, work is happening. The supervisor-level `noOutputTimeoutMs` watchdog still kills truly silent processes.
